_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Swain County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the Great Smoky Mountains county — relatively good record survival despite extreme isolation, but community displacement by GSMNP created unique research challenges.
display_name: Swain County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Bryson City, NC (originally called Charleston; renamed 1889)
previous_courthouses.fate: Original frame courthouse served from county formation. Survived the Civil War period (county formed after the war). Replaced by the 1908 brick building as the logging economy brought growth to Bryson City.

record_losses.cause: Community displacement by Great Smoky Mountains National Park
records_destroyed: community records from displaced settlements (Proctor, Smokemont, Hazel Creek)
school records, church records, and informal community documentation from emptied communities
records_survived: county courthouse records (all survived — no fire or courthouse loss)
record_losses.scope: partial — courthouse records survived but community records were scattered
record_losses.description: Swain County's courthouse records survived intact — there was no major fire or catastrophic loss. However, the displacement of entire communities by the Great Smoky Mountains National Park (1926-1940) created a different kind of record gap. When families were bought out and relocated, the informal community records — church minute books, school rolls, store ledgers, family papers — were scattered. The courthouse records document land purchases by the park commission, but the living history of the displaced communities was fragmented beyond recovery. Additionally, Qualla Boundary (Eastern Band Cherokee) records follow a separate system through the Bureau of Indian Affairs.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The courthouse in Bryson City kept its records, but the communities the park displaced took their informal records with them when they scattered — church minutes, store ledgers, family Bibles moved to a dozen different counties when the park took the land.
record_losses.source: Brown, Margaret Lynn. The Wild East: A Biography of the Great Smoky Mountains. Gainesville: University Press of Florida, 2000.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: Pre-1871 records for the Swain County area are in Jackson and Macon county records (parent counties). Park land acquisition deeds (1926-1940) document the purchase of family farms — these are county records that name families, acreages, and sale prices.
deeds.repository: Swain County Register of Deeds, Bryson City; NC State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Good from formation. Swain County was poor — fewer estates generated probate records than in more prosperous counties.
wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1871 records in parent counties. Some estate records for park-displaced families may have been filed in the counties to which they relocated.
wills_estates.repository: Swain County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.gaps: Pre-1871 marriages in parent county records. Cherokee marriages on the Qualla Boundary follow separate Bureau of Indian Affairs procedures.
marriages.repository: Swain County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.gaps: No significant gaps. Court records document park condemnation proceedings, which name families and describe property.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Swain County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited digitization

vital_records.completeness: Poor until the 1920s. The remote Smoky Mountain communities were among the last in NC to comply with vital records registration. Births in the Oconaluftee valley and along Hazel Creek were frequently unregistered.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1880–1940 (Swain formed 1871; first enumerated 1880). Pre-1871, the area appears in Jackson and Macon county censuses.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The pre-park censuses (1880-1920) document the communities that were later displaced. The 1920 census is the last snapshot of Hazel Creek, Proctor, and Deep Creek as living communities.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Swain County, NC.

substitute_sources.source_type: National Park Service records and park land acquisition files
substitute_sources.description: The NPS and its predecessor commissions documented every land purchase for the Great Smoky Mountains National Park. These files name families, describe properties, list improvements, and record sale prices.
substitute_sources.repository: Great Smoky Mountains National Park archives (Sugarlands); National Archives — Department of the Interior; NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Park acquisition files are a unique resource — they describe family homesteads in detail (house, barn, outbuildings, orchard, acreage, condition) at the moment of displacement. They are effectively detailed property inventories of communities that no longer exist.
substitute_sources.source: Brown, Margaret Lynn. The Wild East: A Biography of the Great Smoky Mountains. Gainesville: University Press of Florida, 2000.

substitute_sources.source_type: Bureau of Indian Affairs / Eastern Band Cherokee records
substitute_sources.description: The Qualla Boundary in Swain County is trust land administered by the Bureau of Indian Affairs. Cherokee enrollment records, allotment records, and tribal census rolls document Eastern Band families separately from county records.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives — BIA records; Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians tribal offices; FamilySearch (some digitized)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For Cherokee families on the Qualla Boundary, BIA records and tribal rolls are the primary genealogical source. These exist parallel to and separate from the county records at Bryson City.
substitute_sources.source: Finger, John R. The Eastern Band of Cherokees, 1819-1900. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1984.

substitute_sources.description: Baptist and Methodist churches in the communities displaced by the park kept minutes and membership rolls. Some of these records traveled with the displaced families; others are at the NC State Archives.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual churches; NC Baptist Historical Collection, Wake Forest University; NC State Archives; GSMNP archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records from displaced communities (Proctor, Hazel Creek, Deep Creek) are among the few records that document those communities' internal social life. Some are irreplaceable.

narrative_hooks: Swain County's courthouse survived where others burned — {ancestor_name}'s deed and court records at Bryson City likely still exist from the county's first year, 1871.

If {ancestor_name}'s family was displaced by the Great Smoky Mountains National Park, the park acquisition files may describe the homestead in more detail than any other surviving record — house, barn, orchard, acreage, and the price the government paid to take it.

For {ancestor_name}'s Cherokee ancestors on the Qualla Boundary, the research path leads not to the Bryson City courthouse but to the Bureau of Indian Affairs records and the tribal rolls of the Eastern Band — a parallel paper trail maintained by a sovereign nation.

The communities the park displaced — Proctor, Smokemont, Hazel Creek — left their records scattered across a dozen counties when the families moved away. {ancestor_name}'s community records may have traveled with the family to wherever they resettled.

Before 1871, {ancestor_name}'s Swain County area records are in Jackson or Macon County — Swain was carved from both, and the paper trail for the first families in the Deep Creek and Oconaluftee valleys begins in those parent courthouses.
source_references: Brown, Margaret Lynn. The Wild East: A Biography of the Great Smoky Mountains. Gainesville: University Press of Florida, 2000.
Finger, John R. The Eastern Band of Cherokees, 1819-1900. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1984.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Inscoe, John C., and Gordon B. McKinney. The Heart of Confederate Appalachia: Western North Carolina in the Civil War. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2004.
